question_title: Will the interest in “ben sasse” change between 2026-04-14 and 2026-04-24 according to Google Trends?
summary: Ben Sasse's Google Trends interest spiked from baseline ~1 to peak 100 on Apr 11, driven by a viral NYT interview about his terminal pancreatic cancer. By Apr 14 (start date), interest has already decayed to 35. Over the next 10 days to Apr 24, standard post-spike decay will return interest to near-baseline levels (1-10), far below the 32 threshold needed for "Doesn't change." Even extremely conservative decay rates (5%/day) produce a value of ~21 by Apr 24. The only meaningful risk to this conclusion is a secondary spike event (most likely Sasse's death announcement), which has ~5-8% probability in the 10-day window but would involve complex rescaling effects. Monte Carlo simulation across decay rates, secondary events, and measurement noise produces 94% Decreases.

factors.description: Post-spike decay dynamics: Interest peaked at 100 on Apr 11, already decaying (49→29→35). Even very slow decay (5%/day) yields value of 21 by Apr 24, well below the 32 threshold. Only <0.9%/day decay would prevent Decreases.
Baseline before spike was ~1. Natural equilibrium for this search term is extremely low. With 10 days of decay, interest should return to 1-10 range, far below 32.
One-time viral story catalyst: Spike driven by single NYT interview/podcast (Apr 9). Not an ongoing developing story. Such one-off spikes typically return to baseline within 1-2 weeks.
Small uptick 29→35 on Apr 13→14 suggests residual follow-up coverage, but modest and within normal post-spike noise.
factors.conditional: If uptick signals sustained interest rather than noise

factors.description: Risk of death announcement: Stage 4 pancreatic cancer, ~4 months past diagnosis with 3-4 month prognosis. ~5-8% chance of death in 10-day window would cause massive spike.
factors.conditional: If Sasse dies during the forecast window

factors.description: Drug trial news: daraxonrasib Phase 3 results expected 'soon' from Revolution Medicines. Could cause moderate interest bump if released during window.
factors.conditional: If drug trial results released in the window

reasoning:
**Forecast: Decreases (94%)**

The core story: Ben Sasse's Google Trends interest spiked massively (baseline ~1 → peak 100) around April 9-11 from a viral NYT interview about his terminal pancreatic cancer. By April 14, interest has already decayed to 35. The question asks whether the value on April 24 will be within ±3 of the April 14 value.

**Key findings:**
- Even at an extremely conservative 5%/day decay rate, the April 24 value would be ~21 — well below the 32 threshold for "Doesn't change"
- To stay in the [32, 38] range requires essentially zero decay (<0.9%/day), which contradicts all empirical evidence from similar news spikes
- The pre-spike baseline was ~1, providing strong gravitational pull downward
- The only meaningful upside risk is a secondary spike event (Sasse's death at ~5-8% probability, or drug trial news), but even these scenarios involve complex rescaling effects that don't reliably produce "Increases"

| Outcome | Probability |
|---------|------------|
| Decreases | **94%** |
| Increases | 4% |
| Doesn't change | 2% |
resolution_criteria: This question resolves “Doesn't change” if the value on the timeline at [https://trends.google.com/trends/explore?geo=US&tz=0&q=ben%20sasse&date=2026-03-25%202026-04-24](https://trends.google.com/trends/explore?geo=US&tz=0&q=ben%20sasse&date=2026-03-25%202026-04-24) for 2026-04-24 is within 3 of the value at 2026-04-14. It resolves “Increases” if the value is more than 3 greater, and resolves “Decreases” if the value is more than 3 lower.
fine_print:
A script will be used to determine the resolution of this question. It will access the data on Google Trends using [SerpApi](https://serpapi.com/), and compare the height of the timeline for the two aforementioned dates. The specific python query will be structured as follows:
```
from serpapi import GoogleSearch

search = GoogleSearch(params={
  "api_key": API_KEY,
  "engine": "google_trends",
  "data_type": "TIMESERIES",
  "geo": "US",
  "tz": 0,
  "q": "ben sasse",
  "date": "2026-03-25 2026-04-24",
})
search.get_dict()
```
Note that there may be differences between the results returned by the API and the data appearing on the page. This seems to be due to the 'tz' parameter not having the intended effect in-browser. In this case, the API results will be considered authoritative.
Note that the precision of the timeline will be 1 day, so this will compare the overall interest for the whole day as determined by Google Trends.
Dates are determined in UTC.
If the data is no longer available, or the script fails, this question will be annulled or manually resolved by a moderator.
